title: Modular 2x4 Workbench
summary: A 4-foot workbench sized around an off-the-shelf 24x48 plywood panel — no plywood ripping, a slatted lower shelf, and a caster option that keeps the same plan mobile.
description:
The dimensioning trick makes this bench: it's designed backwards from the 24" x 48" 3/4" plywood project panel sold off the shelf, so the one sheet-good in the build never touches a saw. Everything else is five 2x4s — two ladder leg sets, top and bottom frames, and a lower shelf slatted from 21" crosspieces.

The assembly sequence is worth copying into other builds: the bottom shelf slats are placed by CENTERS, not measured gaps — find the center of the shelf, center the first slat on it, then center slats in the remaining openings, repeating until full. Zero cumulative spacing error, no arithmetic.

The final step is really a fork in the plan: measure your caster height, cut that much OFF the legs, and the same bench becomes mobile at the same working height. Do it at build time or years later. Pick yellow-toned 2x4s (the plan's note — they're typically drier and lighter than the red-toned ones), and this is a legitimately good first workbench: cheap enough to abuse, sturdy enough to outlast the shop it starts in.

tools.note: All straight crosscuts — the plywood panel is used as purchased.
Transferring the 12" leg marks square across all four legs.

materials.name: 2x4, 10 ft
materials.note: Yellow-toned (pine/spruce) preferred — drier and lighter than red-toned.

materials.name: Plywood project panel, 3/4", 24" x 48"
materials.note: The top, used exactly as purchased — the whole bench is dimensioned around it.

materials.name: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", flat head
Self-tapping wood screws, #8 x 1-1/4", flat head
materials.note: Top attachment (and casters if adding them).

materials.name: Swivel casters, 2", locking
materials.note: Optional — the legs get shortened by the caster height, keeping the bench at working height.

cutList.note: Adding casters? Cut them shorter by your casters' installed height.

steps.title: Build the two leg sets
steps.body: Mark all four legs 12" up from the bottom and square the line across with the speed square. Clamp and attach the legs to the 45" rails with 2-3/4" screws — the 12" line locates the bottom shelf rail. Two identical leg sets.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", flat head

steps.title: Frame the top
steps.body: Screw 21" crosspieces between the leg sets at the top, two screws per joint, forming the top frame.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", flat head

steps.title: Frame the bottom
steps.body: Repeat at the 12" marks for the bottom shelf frame. The bench now stands; check it doesn't rock before filling in the shelf.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", flat head

steps.title: Slat the shelf by centers
steps.body:
Find the center of the bottom shelf opening and the center of one 21" slat; match them and screw down. Then center a slat in each remaining opening, and repeat — every gap self-equalizes, no measuring, no cumulative error.

Steal this method for every slatted thing you ever build.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", flat head

steps.title: Top it — and wheels if you want them
steps.body:
Set the plywood panel on top, adjust the base until the panel's edges match the frame, and screw down with 1-1/4" screws about every 12". The factory panel edges square the bench as a bonus.

Going mobile: measure your casters' installed height, cut exactly that off the legs, and screw the casters to the leg bottoms. Same bench, same height, now it follows the work.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #8 x 1-1/4", flat head

images.alt: Compact 2x4 workbench with plywood top and slatted lower shelf
